lessons.title: Context of C++20
lessons.motivation: Before diving into specific features, it's worth understanding C++20 as a whole: where it stands in the history of C++, what it fundamentally changed, and who this course is for. Without this picture, individual improvements feel like an unrelated collection of additions — and you lose sight of where the language is heading.
lessons.theory:
**C++20 in the history of the standard.** C++20 was officially ratified by ISO in December 2020 as ISO/IEC 14882:2020. It is the largest revision since C++11 — both in the number of features and in their conceptual depth. While C++14 and C++17 were incremental refinements, C++20 introduced four structural reforms simultaneously: the Concepts system, the Ranges library, the Coroutines mechanism, and the Modules system. Any one of these alone would have been a landmark release; all four together make C++20 a paradigm shift.

**Four flagships.** *Concepts* (P0734) finally gave C++ a proper way to express template constraints — replacing 20+ years of SFINAE/enable_if idioms with readable `concept` and `requires` syntax and comprehensible error messages. *Ranges* (P0896) overhauled the entire algorithms library: ranges-aware algorithms, lazy view adapters, pipe syntax, and projections. *Coroutines* (P0912) added native language support for suspendable functions — the mechanism behind async tasks and lazy generators — though without ready-to-use standard types (those came in C++23). *Modules* (P1103) replaced the `#include` preprocessor model with a proper module system, offering faster builds and no macro leakage.

**Target audience.** This course is for developers who know C++17 confidently: structured bindings, `std::optional`/`std::variant`, `if constexpr`, fold expressions, `std::filesystem`. C++14/17 features are not explained — they are the baseline. C++20 is taught completely, from motivation through advanced nuances. Background callouts appear only where a C++17 detail is non-obvious and load-bearing for understanding the specific C++20 feature.

**Course structure.** The course has 11 modules and 33 lessons. Module 0 (this lesson) sets the context. Modules 1–5 cover the flagships and core language changes: language improvements, Concepts, Ranges, Coroutines, Modules. Modules 6–9 cover the library additions: `std::format`, concurrency primitives (`jthread`, `latch`, `barrier`), the expanded `<chrono>` with calendar and timezone support, and low-level utilities (`bit_cast`, `source_location`, `numbers`, `erase_if`). Module 10 wraps up with deprecations and the path to C++23 and C++26. Each lesson is theory and examples — mark-as-read tracks your progress.

**C++20 in practice.** By mid-2026, C++20 support is broad: GCC 10+, Clang 10+, and MSVC 19.28+ cover the vast majority of features in this course. Concepts and Ranges have the highest immediate ROI — they integrate into existing C++17 codebases without breaking changes and produce better code today. Coroutines require a supporting library or hand-rolled promise types (C++23's `std::generator` solves this). Modules are still challenging in build systems and are covered last for that reason. The recommended entry points for production adoption: Concepts first, then Ranges + format, then concurrency primitives.
